segments.s571-g41: بگفت is the simple past reporting verb 'he said'. آنک is a relative/connector meaning 'that/which' introducing the content of what was said. از + noun here marks the material/source ('of/from'); چرم خر is a genitival phrase 'donkey's leather' (چرم = leather, خر = donkey). دیده بود is a perfect/past construction: دیده (past participle 'seen') + بود (past auxiliary) = 'had seen'. Together the segment means 'he said that he had seen (it) of/from a donkey's hide.'
segments.s571-g42: سخنهای قیصر is a noun phrase 'the words/sayings of Caesar' (سخنهای = plural/indefinite 'sayings/words', قیصر = Caesar/emperor). که introduces a relative clause 'that/which'; بشنیده بود again uses the past participle بشنیده ('heard') with the auxiliary بود to give 'had heard'. The clause modifies 'سخنهای قیصر' — 'the words of Caesar which he had heard.'
segments.s571-g43: هم here means 'also' or 'too', adding this remark to the previous ones. آزادی is a noun 'freedom/release' and is the object/topic of بگفت that follows. آن بت خوب‌چهر is a fixed descriptive phrase: آن = 'that', بت in poetic usage = 'beloved/beauty' (literally 'idol'), modified by خوب‌چهر 'fair‑faced'. Word order is determiner + noun + adjective (common in Persian poetry).
segments.s571-g44: بگفت is again the past reporting verb 'he said/recounted'. آنچ (آن‌چ) means 'that which' or 'what', introducing a clause of content. او is the third‑person pronoun 'she/he' (here 'she' for the beloved), and کرد is the simple past 'did' (to do/make). پیدا modifies the verb as 'apparent/plainly' (pیدا here = 'visible/manifest'). ز مهر = 'out of love' (ز = poetic 'from/because of', مهر = 'love/affection'). Together: 'he recounted what she had openly done out of love.'
segments.s571-g45: کزو is a fused prepositional/relative form 'from whom/from which'. یافتم is the first‑person past 'I obtained/found' (یافتم = 'I got'). جان 'life/soul' is the direct object. و connects to the following clause. از کردگار = 'from the Creator/God' where از = 'from' and کردگار = 'Creator/God'. The segment thus gives 'from whom I obtained life, and from the Creator ...'.
segments.s571-g46: که introduces an optative/parenthetical wish here: 'that/may'. فرخنده is an adjective 'auspicious/fortunate'. بادا is the classical optative particle meaning 'may it be'. برو روزگار is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'upon the days / in the time' — together 'فرخنده بادا برو روزگار' = 'may the times be auspicious'. (Note: برو here participates in the fixed poetic expression and is not a literal 'go' in modern prose.)
segments.s571-g47: وگر is the conditional 'if' (or 'but if'). شهریاری is an abstract noun 'kingship/sovereignty' or 'a royal position'. و = 'and'. فرخنده‌ای is an indefinite/qualifying phrase 'an auspicious/fortunate one' (the ruler described as fortunate). The segment sets a conditional frame 'and if there were kingship and a fortunate (ruler)'.
segments.s571-g48: بود is the past copula 'there was'/'was'. بنده here functions as humble self‑reference 'servant' (a common self‑effacing term in Persian). پرهنر is an adjective 'full of art/skill' = 'cultured, skilled'. بنده‌ای repeats the humble form with the indefinite/affective suffix -ای, emphasizing 'a (humble) servant.' The clause says essentially 'there was a skilled/cultured servant — a humble servant.'
segments.s571-g49: منم is the fused colloquial/literary form 'I am' (من + ام). بنده here is a self‑referential humble noun ('servant') following the copula. این مهربان بنده را is a single noun phrase in apposition (this kindly/benevolent person), with را marking that phrase as definite/object (emphatic); the second بنده is the referent (the person served). The whole segment therefore reads: 'I am (the) servant of this kind person.'
segments.s571-g50: گشاده‌دل و نازپرورده are adjectival descriptors placed after the noun phrase to qualify that same person (literary apposition). The conjunction و simply links the two adjectives. The final را is the same definite/object marker tying these modifiers to the previously mentioned person — a poetic repetition for emphasis.
segments.s571-g51: ز (poetic variant of از) = 'from'. هر سو = 'every side/quarter' (هر + سو). که introduces a relative clause here meaning 'where'. اکنون = 'now'. سپاه = 'army'. منست is an archaic/literary predicate corresponding to است or 'is/stands/has been set' (here describing the army as being present/arrayed on those sides). Word order is typical literary Persian with the relative clause linking the locative phrase to the verb.
segments.s571-g52: وگر is a conditional/contrastive particle ('and if' / 'or if' / 'whether'). پادشاهی = 'kingship/kingdom'. و = 'and'. راه = 'way/road'. منست again is the literary copular/predicative form meaning 'is/stands/has been arranged' — here implying control or order over the kingship/road. The clause links back to the previous locative sense: whether it is a kingdom or a road, it is (so) arranged/held.
segments.s571-g53: همه کس = 'everyone' (همه + کس). فرستید is a 2nd‑person plural imperative 'send' (literary command). و = 'and'. آگه کنید is another imperative phrase 'make (them) aware / notify' where آگه (literary 'aware/notice') functions as the object of کنید ('do/make'), so together 'inform/notify'.
segments.s571-g54: طلایه = 'vanguard' (the leading troops). پراگنده = 'scattered/widespread', an adjectival qualifier. بر + ره = 'on/toward the road' (بر here with the sense 'toward/on' heading the direction), and کنید is again the imperative 'do/make' used here to mean 'place/deploy/send' — 'deploy the scattered vanguard onto the road.'
segments.s571-g55: ببندید is a 2nd‑person plural imperative 'close/lock/shut off' (command to block). ویژه here means 'especially' / 'particularly' qualifying which route to close. ره = 'road/way'. طیسفون is the proper name 'Ctesiphon' (a city). Together: 'Close (it), particularly the road to Ctesiphon.'
segments.s571-g56: نباید is an impersonal modal 'must not / should not' (prohibitive). که introduces the content clause 'that...'. آگاهی = 'news/information' (here: news). آید is the present subjunctive/indicative of 'to come' (آمَد) — 'comes' or 'reach'. برون = 'out/forth'. The clause thus states the prohibition: 'news must not come out.'
segments.s571-g25: سوی is the preposition 'toward' placed before its object; سورستان here is a place-name (not the common noun 'banquet‑ground'). سربرافراختند is a past plural verb (سر + بر افراختند) meaning 'they raised their heads / showed themselves' — a poetic, active past-tense clause whose verb follows the prepositional phrase.
segments.s571-g26: یگان و دوگانه is a coordinated description 'single and double' (i.e. one- and two‑deep or one- and two‑rank formations). همی is the archaic/progressive particle used in epic poetry to mark continuous or emphatic action, and تاختند is the past plural verb 'they charged/attacked'. Together they mean 'they were charging/they charged'.
segments.s571-g27: به is the directional preposition 'to/into'; درگاه here means 'threshold/doorway' (a place marker) and پالیزبان is the addressee/possessor 'the gardener'. آمدند is the past plural 'they came'. The whole clause is a simple locative motion: 'they came to the gardener's threshold'.
segments.s571-g28: به شادی is an adverbial phrase 'with joy / rejoicing'. بر here functions as a directional preposition 'toward / to' (poetic usage) and میزبان is 'the host'; آمدند (past plural) repeats 'they came'. The clause therefore reads 'rejoicing, they approached the host.'
segments.s571-g29: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; لشکر is the subject 'the army'. شد is the past of شدن 'became' and combines with the adjective آسوده 'at peace/settled' to form a result/state 'became settled'. بر + درسرای is a locative phrase 'at/on the inn/house' (poetic placement after the predicate).
segments.s571-g30: This clause shows a common poetic inversion: the prepositional phrase به نزدیک شاه آمد (verb phrase 'came near the king') comes before the subject آن پاک‌رای ('that virtuous one'). آمد is past singular 'came', and آن پاک‌رای is the demonstrative + epithet identifying who approached the king.
segments.s571-g31: به شاه جهان is the indirect object 'to the king of the world'; گفت is the verb 'said' (here used in past tense), and پس میزبان places the speaker after the verb in the order 'then the host' — a poetic word order meaning 'then the host said to the king'.
segments.s571-g32: خجستست is an older/poetic form equivalent to خجسته است 'is auspicious/fortunate'. بر here is used poetically in a comparative or evaluative sense ('over/above/than' the usual literal 'on'), ماه is 'the moon' (a standard poetic simile for beauty/brightness), and پالیزبان names the gardener being praised. The clause reads as a short encomium: 'The gardener is blessed/fortunate as (or even more than) the moon.'
segments.s571-g57: چو here functions as a temporal/conditional conjunction meaning “when” or “if,” placed at the clause front. قیصر is the subject that precedes the verb; بیابد is a present/subjunctive-form verb meaning “comes to know/learn” (used here with a potential/conditional flavor). ز ما آگهی is a prepositional phrase (ز = “from,” ما = “us,” آگهی = “news/information”), with the preposition before its possessor in poetic word order.
segments.s571-g58: که introduces the content clause (“that …”). بیدار is an adjective/participial notion “awakened/roused,” and شد is the past/become auxiliary here (“became/was”), so بیدار شد is a past-state: “was roused/awakened.” فر شاهنشهی is a noun phrase with فر (the divine/royal glory, farr) as head and شاهنشهی (of the king of kings / imperial) as its qualifier.
segments.s571-g59: بیاید is an optative/potential form meaning roughly “may come” / “should come” (classical/poetic usage conveying possibility). سپاه is the subject (“an army/host”). م + را here represent the object clitic (م = enclitic for “me,” را = definite/object marker); they mark سپاه’s action as directed at “me.” برکند is a transitive verb in the potential/subjunctive sense “uproot/dislodge” and takes the object marked by را.
segments.s571-g60: دل و پشت is a coordinated noun phrase ‘heart and back’ (two parallel objects). ایرانیان is the genitive/possessive group that these nouns belong to (‘of the Iranians’). بشکند is a present/subjunctive or optative form meaning “(will) break” or “be shattered,” used here to state the feared result.
segments.s571-g61: کنون is an adverb ‘now’ placed initially. ما نداریم is the negative present: ما (we) + نداریم (‘do not have’). پایاب اوی is a noun with an archaic/poetic possessive enclitic اوی = “his”; پایاب is a substantive here (a kind of footing/support or qanat-outlet in literal sense) — the phrase means that they no longer possess that support of his.
segments.s571-g62: نه is a negation placed before the verb (not). پیچیم is 1st-person-plural present/subjunctive ‘we turn/turn to’ (here idiomatically ‘we cannot turn to / we cannot rely upon’). با is the preposition ‘with’; بخت شاداب اوی is a noun phrase ‘his (اوی) flourishing/vigorous fortune’ with the adjective شاداب following بخت (noun + adjective). Together the clause says they cannot take refuge in or depend on his fortunate vigor.
segments.s571-g63: چو introduces the temporal/conditional clause “when/if.” موبد is the subject (mōbed, Zoroastrian priest). بیاید is a modal/optative ‘may come’ or simple ‘comes.’ بیارد is used here as a verb meaning ‘brings’ (poetic/archaic form of ‘bring’); the following سپاه is the thing brought (an army/host). Word order is Subject – Verb – Object.
segments.s571-g64: ز لشکر is a prepositional/agent phrase (‘from/of the host/army’ — z is the poetic form of از). ببندیم is 1st-person-plural subjunctive/jussive used as a hortative/future: ‘we will close/block/ambush.’ بر is a preposition ‘upon/against’; پشه راه is the object of that preposition (راه = ‘way/path’; پشه is a modifier in the compound — here a poetic expression referring to a narrow or insignificant approach), so the clause says they will bar/close the way against the army.
segments.s571-g33: سپاه انجمن شد is a nominal clause: سپاه (subject) + انجمن شد (became an assembly/gathered). شد is the past-tense auxiliary/verb 'became' here. بدین درسرای is a locative phrase: بدین (poetic contraction meaning 'به + این', 'at/with this') + درسرای 'in the palace/court' giving the place of assembly.
segments.s571-g34: نگه کن is an imperative construction: نگه (look/watch) + the light imperative کن 'do!'. کنون is the adverb 'now'. تا introduces a subordinate/purpose clause here ('so that / in order that') and governs چه آیدت رای 'what counsel comes to you' — آیدت is 3rd-person present/subjunctive-like verb 'comes' with the enclitic -ت 'to you', and رای is the noun 'counsel/opinion' serving as the thing that may arrive.
segments.s571-g35: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb 'he ordered / he said'. تا here introduces the intended/result clause 'so that / that' and is followed by برگشادند (past, 3rd-person plural) 'they opened' with راه 'the way' as the object: he ordered that the way(s) be opened.
segments.s571-g36: اگر چه is a concessive phrase 'although / though' (literally 'if that'), introducing a contrast. فرومایه is an adjective 'lowly / base' describing the people/thing implied; بد is an intensifying adjective/adverb 'bad / ill' and جایگاه is 'place / station' — together: 'though (they were) base and in an ill place.' The subject is omitted and understood from the prior clause.
segments.s571-g37: چو رفتند is a temporal clause 'when they went' (چو = when, رفتند = they went). نزدیک آن نامجوی is an adverbial phrase 'near that Namjuy/name-seeker' where آن is the demonstrative 'that' and نامجوی functions as a noun/epithet (here 'name-seeker' or a proper epithet). Word order places the location phrase after the verb.
segments.s571-g38: یکایک 'one by one' modifies the verb; نهادند is the past plural of نهادن 'they placed / put' (here reflexively used as 'they cast/laid themselves'). بر خاک روی is a frozen poetic sequence meaning 'upon the dust/ground (with) face' — i.e. 'they laid their faces on the ground.' The preposition بر 'on' governs خاک 'earth/dust', while روی 'face' names what was laid.
segments.s571-g39: مهان را همه is the object phrase 'all the guests' marked by the object marker را; شاه (the king) is the subject. In Persian poetry the object can precede the subject for emphasis. در بر گرفت is an idiomatic verb phrase ('گرفت' = took/seized) with در بر = 'into his arms' — together 'he took/embraced (them).' The verb is past tense 3rd singular.
segments.s571-g40: ز بدها is a prepositional phrase 'from / because of the bad ones' (ز = poetic 'from/of'). خروشیدن is the infinitive/verbal noun 'to roar / to cry out'. اندر گرفت is a light-verb construction: گرفتن with an adverbial اندر 'into' means 'took to (doing something)' — so together 'he took to roaring' or 'began to cry out' because of the wicked.
segments.s571-g65: بسازیم and کنیم are present-subjunctive/optative 1st-person-plural forms (the b‑prefix marks the subjunctive/imperative in classical Persian), here functioning like English “let us…/we should…”. The clause uses two near‑synonymous verb phrases (بسا‌زیم and آرایشی نو کنیم) for emphasis: آرایشی نو is a noun phrase (آرایشی = ‘array/arrangement’) with the adjective نو postposed, and the verb کنیم completes the object phrase in the usual Persian order (object before verb).
segments.s571-g66: نهانی is an adverb ‘secretly/stealthily’ modifying the action; مگر introduces an exception/conditional here and means ‘unless’. باغ is the direct object, followed by the adjective بی‌خو (‘sleepless’) and then the verb کنیم (‘we make’), so باغ بی‌خو کنیم = ‘we make the garden sleepless’. Grammatically this whole clause is the exception attached to the preceding optative (‘let us…unless…’).
segments.s571-g67: بباید is a modal/impersonal form meaning ‘it is necessary’ or ‘one must’; here it governs the rest of the clause. The locative prepositional phrase به هر گوشه‌ای (‘at/to every corner’) comes before the noun دیده‌بان (‘watchman/sentinel’), so the structure reads ‘there must be a sentinel at every corner’ (typical Persian ordering: locative phrase + noun).
segments.s571-g68: This is a coordinated specification of the kinds of watchers: طلایه به‌روز = ‘vanguard/advance guard by day’ and به شب پاسبان = ‘and a guard by night’. Here به‌روز and به شب are temporal/qualifying uses of به (‘in/at/by [the time of]’), and پاسبان/طلایه are nouns appositive to دیده‌بان in the previous segment (they describe the watcher’s role by day and by night).
segments.s571-g69: ازان پس is a temporal phrase ‘from that time on / after that’. نمانیم is the verb مانْدن in a form used here in narrative to mean ‘there remained / were left’ (classical usage can employ such forms impersonally to report that none remained). از رومیان is a partitive/genitive phrase ‘of the Romans’ indicating who is affected: ‘none of the Romans remained’.
segments.s571-g70: کسی with a negative or negating context functions as ‘no one’ here. خسپد is an archaic/poetic verb meaning ‘to sleep / lie down’ (not listed among the provided senses for this lemma), and it is followed by the predicative adjectives ایمن (safe) and گشاده‌میان (open‑faced / carefree) describing the manner of sleeping. Together the clause says that no one slept in safety and with a carefree face.
segments.s571-g1: چو is an archaic/poetic subordinating conjunction meaning 'when' that introduces a temporal clause. پالیزبان گفت is a simple past clause 'the gardener spoke'; و coordinates the following clause; موبد شنید is another past clause 'the mobed heard' with the same implied time. Both verbs are 3rd person past simple and the subject of the second clause (موبد) is explicit.
segments.s571-g2: به introduces the following noun phrase as the object/target of perception; روشن‌روان is an epithet 'bright‑soul' placed before the head noun مرد (a man/person), with the adjective دانا following the noun (مرد دانا = 'the wise man'). بدید is the 3rd person past of دیدن 'saw' and its subject is understood from the previous clause (the mobed). The word order is poetic but reads 'he saw the bright‑souled, wise man.'
segments.s571-g3: که introduces a content clause 'that...' or can function as a relative; آن شیردل مرد is the noun phrase 'that lion‑hearted man'. جز ... نیست is an idiomatic construction meaning 'is none other than' or 'is only'; here 'جز شاه نیست' gives the sense 'he is nothing but (i.e. essentially) the king.' The verb نیست (is not) appears at the end as the negation completing the idiom.
segments.s571-g4: همان is emphatic 'that very' or 'the same'; چهر او means 'his visage/face'. The phrase جز در گاه نیست again uses the 'جز ... نیست' pattern to mean 'is only/exists only in...' Here در گاه (the two words together, poetic spacing) functions as درگاه 'the (royal) court' — so the clause says his face/visage is only (found) in the court. نیسْت closes the negation as in the idiom.
segments.s571-g5: فرستاده‌ای is an indefinite/nominal form 'a messenger' (here used as the subject); جست is the past of جستن and in this context means 'sought out' or 'went in search of' (i.e. he sought Roshan‑ravan). روشن‌روان is the person sought (a proper epithet/name). The clause is compact and poetic: 'a messenger sought out Roshan‑ravan.'
segments.s571-g6: فرستاد is the 3rd person past 'sent'; موبد, the agent, follows the verb (verb–subject inversion common in poetry) so the reading is 'the mobed sent'. بر here marks direction/recipient 'to/toward' and پهلوان is the destination/recipient 'the champion/hero.' The implied object of فرستاد is the messenger mentioned before.
segments.s571-g7: که introduces a clause 'that/when'; پیدا شد is a copular/perceptual phrase 'became visible/appeared' (پیدا 'apparent' + شد 'became/was'); آن فر شاپور شاه is the noun phrase 'that farr (divine royal glory) of Shapur the king' with فر (farr) a technical/poetic term for royal radiance. The clause means 'when that glory of King Shapur appeared.'
segments.s571-g8: تو is the vocative/imperative addressee 'you' (thou); از هر سوی is a prepositional phrase 'from every side/direction' (از 'from', هر 'every', سوی 'side/direction'); انجمن کن سپاه is an imperative sequence where انجمن کردن 'to assemble' is expressed by noun+کن (کن = imperative 'do'), so the meaning is 'assemble/muster the army.' Word order places the pronoun and directional phrase before the verb phrase for emphasis.
segments.s571-g17: که here begins a subordinate/relative clause ('who' / 'that'). دانست is the simple past 'knew' (3rd person), and هرگز with دانست creates the rhetorical sense 'who could ever have known/imagined'. The second که introduces the content of that clause ('that...') and is immediately followed by the subject شاپور شاه ('King Shapur').
segments.s571-g18: ببیند is the present/subjunctive/irrealis form 'would see' or 'might see' used in a past narrative to express what was imagined. سپه (army) is the object; نیز 'also' qualifies it. و links a short elliptical clause: او را سپاه repeats 'army' with او را (the pronoun plus را used for emphasis/definiteness), so the clause effectively says 'and his army' (the verb is omitted by ellipsis). The object marker را with the pronoun emphasises possession/definiteness.
segments.s571-g19: سپاس is the noun 'thanks' and از تو literally 'to/for you' (here: 'thanks to you' or 'I thank you'). ای marks the vocative ('O'), inserted after تو to address directly. دادگر is an epithet 'just' (applied vocatively). یک خدای is a descriptive title 'one God' or 'the one God' modifying the vocative.
segments.s571-g20: جهاندار is another epithet, 'sovereign (of the world)', and و links the next phrase. بر نیکویی رهنمای is a prepositional phrase with رهنمای 'guide' as the predicative/epithet and بر نیکویی 'toward goodness' (بر + noun) giving its direction; the whole line lists titles/attributes addressed to God.
segments.s571-g21: چو = 'when' (temporal conjunction). شب is the time/subject 'night'. برکشید is the past tense 'raised' (3rd person). آن درفش سیاه 'that black banner' is the direct object; the order here (temporal marker + verb + object) reflects poetic inversion rather than modern prose order.
segments.s571-g22: ستاره پدید آمد is a light-verb phrase 'a star appeared' (پدید آمد = 'came into view, appeared'). از گرد ماه is a prepositional phrase 'from around the moon' where گرد means 'around' (not 'dust') and follows the verb phrase in normal poetic order.
segments.s571-g23: فراز آمد is a poetic verb phrase 'rose up' or 'came up on high' (فراز here adverbial/verb complement). از هر سوی 'from every direction/side' indicates source, and لشکری ('a host/army') is the noun denoting the forces that moved — the order is verb phrase + source + noun, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s571-g24: به جایی 'to a place' introduces the destination; که begins a relative clause 'where'. بد 'bad/evil' placed before در جهان ('in the world') and followed by مهتری ('preeminence') yields 'where evil was preeminent in the world' — مهتری functions as the nominal predicate meaning 'superiority/dominance'.
segments.s571-g9: فرستاده موبد is a genitive noun phrase ("the messenger of the mōbed"). آمد is the simple past verb "came." دوان is an adverbial participle/adverb (poetic) placed after the verb to qualify the manner: "came running/rushing." Word order is subject — past verb — manner adverb, a common narrative pattern in classical Persian.
segments.s571-g10: ز جایی که introduces a relative/locative clause: ز (from) + جایی (a place/somewhere) + که (that/where). بد is an adjective "bad/unpleasant" used here metaphorically (see note) and تا functions as "as far as / even," linking to در پهلوان where در is the physical "door/threshold" sense and پهلوان means "the champion/hero." The whole clause reads literally "from a place that badness reached even to the champion's door," i.e. news (or an effect) reached the champion's threshold.
segments.s571-g11: بگفت is a narrative past verb meaning "he said" (ب- prefix + گفت is an older/narrative past form). آنک is a classical conjunction/relative pronoun meaning "that" (introducing reported content). در باغ شادی و بخت is a locative prepositional phrase: در (in) + باغ (garden) + شادی و بخت ("joy and fortune/fortune" joined by و).
segments.s571-g12: شکفته شد is a two-word verbal idea: شکفته (blossomed, participial/adjectival) + شد (here functioning as an auxiliary/passive marker — "became/was"). The noun phrase آن خسروانی درخت follows in inverted (poetic) order: آن (the/that) + خسروانی (royal/Khosrowian) + درخت (tree). In natural English one reads ‘‘the royal tree had blossomed.’’
segments.s571-g13: سپهبد is the subject (the spahbed/commander). ز here means "because of/from" and takes the noun that follows (گفتار او) as the cause: گفتار او = "his words/speech." گشت شاد is a simple past/resultative: گشت (became) + شاد (happy), i.e. "became glad."
segments.s571-g14: This clause has possessive morphology and two parallel predicates. دلش = دل + -ش (his heart). پر ز کین گشت = پر (full) + ز (of/from) + کین (hatred/vengeance) + گشت (became) — "his heart became full of hatred." The conjunction و links the next phrase لب پر ز باد: لب (lip) + پر (full) + ز (of) + باد (literally "wind"); here باد is metaphorical (breath, exhalation, or figurative utterance). The adjectives (پر) follow the possessed noun, a common poetic order.
segments.s571-g15: به دادار گفت is a dative + verb: به (to) + دادار (the Creator) + گفت (said). ای جهاندار راست is a vocative phrase addressing the ruler: ای (O) + جهاندار (world-holder/sovereign) + راست (true/just/right) — a classical, elevated form of address.
segments.s571-g16: پرستش کنی is a subordinate clause with the present-subjunctive form کنی (you do / that one do) used in indirect speech to render the act "to worship"; syntactically it reads as "(to) worship." جز ترا is an exception phrase: جز (except) + ترا (you, object form) meaning "except you." ناسزاست is a fused predicate (ناسزا + است) meaning "it is an insult/an indignity/unsuitable." Together: "To worship anyone except you is an indignity to you."

sentences.s571-s21: He said what he had seen of a donkey's hide and the words of Caesar that he had heard.
sentences.s571-s22: He also spoke of the release of that fair‑faced beloved, recounting what she had plainly done out of love.
sentences.s571-s23: From whom I obtained my life — and may the Creator make the times auspicious.
sentences.s571-s24: And if there was kingship and an auspicious ruler, there was a cultured servant — a humble servant.
sentences.s571-s25: I am the servant of this gracious one — broad‑hearted and pampered.
sentences.s571-s26: From every quarter where my army now is — and whether it is kingship or a road, it is arranged/held.
sentences.s571-s27: Send everyone and notify them; deploy the scattered vanguard along the road.
sentences.s571-s28: Shut it up — especially the road to Ctesiphon; news must not get out.
sentences.s571-s13: They moved toward Sourastan and raised their heads; in single and double ranks they charged.
sentences.s571-s14: They came to the gardener's threshold; rejoicing, they came before the host.
sentences.s571-s15: When the army had settled at the inn, that virtuous man came near the king.
sentences.s571-s16: Then the host said to the king of the world: 'The gardener is blessed as the moon.'
sentences.s571-s29: If/when Caesar learns of us — that the Shahanshah's royal farr has been roused.
sentences.s571-s30: An army may come and uproot me, and it will break the hearts and backs of the Iranians.
sentences.s571-s31: Now we have no (secure) footing of his, nor can we rely on his flourishing fortune.
sentences.s571-s32: If/when the mobed comes and brings an army, we will shut them off from the host and block the road.
sentences.s571-s17: The host assembled in this palace; watch now to see what counsel comes to you.
sentences.s571-s18: He commanded that the way be opened, though (they were) lowly and ill-placed.
sentences.s571-s19: When they went near that name-seeker, one by one they laid their faces on the ground.
sentences.s571-s20: The king embraced all the guests, and, because of the wicked ones, began to cry out.
sentences.s571-s33: Let us build and set a new formation in secret — unless, that is, we make the garden sleepless.
sentences.s571-s34: There must be a watchman at every corner — a vanguard by day and a guard by night.
sentences.s571-s35: After that none of the Romans remained; no one would sleep safe and carefree.
sentences.s571-s1: When the gardener spoke and the mobed heard, he saw the bright‑souled, wise man.
sentences.s571-s2: That lion‑hearted man is none other than the king; that very visage of his is found only in the royal court.
sentences.s571-s3: A messenger sought out Roshan‑ravan; the mobed sent him to the champion.
sentences.s571-s4: When that farr (royal glory) of King Shapur became manifest, you muster troops from every quarter.
sentences.s571-s9: Who could ever have imagined that King Shapur would behold an army — and that an army would belong to him as well?
sentences.s571-s10: Thanks to you — O just one, single God, sovereign of the world and guide toward goodness.
sentences.s571-s11: When night raised that black standard, a star appeared from around the moon.
sentences.s571-s12: From every side a host advanced on high toward a place where evil held preeminence in the world.
sentences.s571-s5: The mobed's envoy came running from a place whose news had reached even the champion's threshold.
sentences.s571-s6: He said that in the garden of joy and fortune the royal tree had blossomed.
sentences.s571-s7: The spahbed, because of his words, was glad; his heart became full of hatred and his lip full of wind.
sentences.s571-s8: He said to the Creator, 'O Sovereign of the world! To worship anyone other than you would be an affront to you.'
